title: Chapter 1 - Version Control, Git, and GitHub
description: Quick concept check on Git, GitHub, and version control.

questions.prompt: What is the main purpose of version control?

options.label: Track changes and keep history of files
Automatically fix bugs in your code

explanations.overview: Version control keeps a history of changes so you can review and undo work safely.

byOptionId.a: Not quite. Version control is about tracking history, not performance.
byOptionId.b: Not quite. Version control tracks changes and keeps history.
byOptionId.c: Correct. Folders still exist; version control tracks what changes inside them.
byOptionId.d: Not quite. Git can help you undo changes, but it does not fix bugs automatically.

options.label: Git is a website, and GitHub is a tool on your computer
Git is a tool, and GitHub is a website that hosts repos
Git and GitHub are the exact same thing
You must use GitHub to use Git

explanations.overview: Git runs on your computer; GitHub is a popular place to store and share Git repositories online.

byOptionId.b: Correct. Git is local; GitHub is online hosting and collaboration.
byOptionId.c: Incorrect. They are related, but not the same.
byOptionId.d: Incorrect. You can use Git locally without GitHub.

questions.prompt: In Git, what is a commit?

options.label: A file that deletes old code
A saved snapshot of your project with a message

explanations.overview: A commit records a point-in-time snapshot of your project, plus a message describing it.

byOptionId.a: Not quite. A commit is not a folder.
byOptionId.b: Not quite. A commit is not a password.
byOptionId.c: Not quite. A commit is not a file, and it does not delete old code.
byOptionId.d: Correct. A commit is a snapshot with a message and an ID.

explanations.overview: A repository is the folder (and its history) that Git tracks.

byOptionId.a: Correct. A repo is a folder tracked by Git.
byOptionId.b: Incorrect. Repos are not temporary random files.
byOptionId.d: Incorrect. Repos are not programming languages.
